get.description:
Retrieves a list of payment methods. This endpoint returns payment method details including billing information,
payment gateway details, and status.

post.description:
Creates a payment method against a specific customer.

This endpoint is available at:
`https://{site}.subscriptionflow.com/api/v1/payment-methods`

- **parent_type**: must always be `"Customer"`.
- **parent_id**: must be set to the corresponding customer ID.

default.description: If you want to set this as the default payment method, set to 1. Example: 1

post.description:
Update the `default` flag and/or `status` of a payment method. Use this to manage the customer's default card and to enable or disable a saved method.

**Auth:** `Authorization: Bearer <token>` — requires the `access-global-api` scope.

**Behavior:**
- Promoting a method to default (`default: true`) clears the `default` flag on every other payment method for the same `parent_id` + `parent_type`.
- Setting `status` to `Inactive` on the current default unsets `default` on that record.
- A method cannot be set as default unless its effective status (after the update) is `Active`.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: Payment method UUID.

schema.description: At least one of `default` or `status` must be provided.

default.description: `true` / `false`. Setting `true` requires the resulting status to be `Active`.

status.description: Changing to `Inactive` on a default method automatically clears default.

200.description: OK — Returns the updated payment method (GeneralResource).

schema.description: GeneralResource wrapping the payment method resource.

404.description: Not Found — Payment method ID does not exist.

422.description: Unprocessable Entity — Validation failure on `default` / `status`, neither field provided, or `default: true` while resulting status is not `Active`.

500.description: Internal Server Error — Unexpected failure while saving.

tags.name: Getting Started
tags.description: SubscriptionFlow provides an HTTP-based API that follows the principles of **REST**. The HTTP rules followed allow simple HTTP clients like cURL to be used as well. The URLs are resource-oriented, the request format is form-encoded, and the responses are in JSON. Depending on the type of operation, the endpoints use one of the following HTTP methods: - **GET**: Used for all read-only operations such as retrieving a resource or listing a group of resources. - **POST**: Used for all create operations. Such operations creates a resource. - **PUT**: Used for all update operations. Such operations modify the state of a resource. - **DELETE**: Used for all remove operations. Such as removing of a resource. # Watch Our Guide Videos --- # API Sequence Flow ### Subscription Creation Flow in SubscriptionFlow Follow this sequence to create a subscription in SubscriptionFlow: #### Step 1: Authentication First, you need to authenticate using your **client_id** and **client_secret**. This authentication step generates an **access token**, which is required to authorize all API requests that follow. --- #### Step 2: Create Customer After authentication, you need to create a customer in SubscriptionFlow. The customer represents the end user or business that will own the subscription, billing, and invoices. --- #### Step 3: Attach Payment Method to Customer Once the customer is created, you need to attach a **payment method** to the customer. The payment method is used for charging invoices and processing recurring payments for subscriptions. --- #### Step 4: Create Products and Attach Plans Next, you need to create **products**. Products represent the services or items you want to sell. Once the product is created, you must attach one or more **plans** to it. Plans define pricing, billing frequency, and renewal rules for the product. --- #### Step 5: Create Subscription After the customer, payment method, product, and plan are created, you can create a **subscription**. The subscription links the customer with the selected product and plan. Once created, SubscriptionFlow automatically manages billing, invoicing, and renewals based on the plan configuration. --- # Using Postman for API Testing We recommend using [Postman](https://www.postman.com/) for seamless testing and interaction with our APIs. To make your testing process even smoother, we have prepared a comprehensive collection of API requests in Postman. [](https://www.postman.com/subscriptionflow/workspace/subscriptionflow/overview) Click the link above to import our collection into your Postman workspace. This collection includes pre-configured requests for various API endpoints, allowing you to easily explore and test the functionalities of our API. # API vs Webhooks ### When to Use API vs Webhooks in SubscriptionFlow SubscriptionFlow provides both **APIs** and **Webhooks** so you can integrate customer, subscription, and payment events into your systems. This guide helps you decide when to use **APIs**, **Webhooks**, or a combination of both. --- ### 🔹 Use APIs when you need to: - **Pull data on demand** *Example:* A CRM system fetches the latest subscription status only when a sales rep opens a customer profile. - **Create or update resources** *Example:* Your checkout flow calls the API to create a subscription and generate an invoice when a customer signs up. - **Run scheduled reports or syncs** *Example:* A nightly script uses the API to fetch all payments collected today and exports them to your accounting system. 👉 **API = You request the data when you need it.** --- ### 🔹 Use Webhooks when you need to: - **Receive real-time notifications** *Example:* SubscriptionFlow sends a webhook the moment a payment fails, so you can email the customer automatically. - **Trigger downstream workflows** *Example:* A subscription renewal webhook extends customer access in your app. - **Reduce polling** *Example:* Instead of checking the API every 5 minutes for invoices, SubscriptionFlow sends a webhook whenever one is created. 👉 **Webhooks = SubscriptionFlow notifies you when something happens.** --- ### 🔹 Use Both together when you need: - **Real-time + detailed data** *Example:* A webhook notifies your system that an invoice was paid → your system calls the API to fetch full invoice details for your ERP. - **Error handling** *Example:* If your system was down and missed a cancellation webhook, you can later reconcile with the API. 👉 **Both = Reliable, real-time, and detailed integrations.** --- ### 📌 Best Practice - Use **Webhooks** for events (*triggers*). - Use **APIs** for data retrieval and actions (*fetch, create, update*). - Combine both for scalable, real-time, and accurate integrations. --- ### ⚡ Example Scenarios Recap - **API Only** → Create a subscription when a customer signs up. - **Webhook Only** → Get notified instantly when a payment fails. - **Both Together** → Webhook triggers on invoice payment → API fetches invoice details for reporting.

tags.name: Authentication
tags.description:
SubscriptionFlow uses authentication tokens for authorization of API requests. Follow these steps to obtain an authentication token:

1. **Client Credentials**: To get started, follow our [Client Credentials Guide]
(https://support.subscriptionflow.com/hc/en-us/articles/900004667566-How-To-Use-OAuth-Client)
to obtain your unique `client_id` and `client_secret` from your SubscriptionFlow (SF) instance.

2. **Obtain an Auth Token**: Once you have your `client_id` and `client_secret`,
make a POST request to the OAuth endpoint with these credentials to obtain a bearer token.
Your request should include the following:
   - `client_id`: Your client ID.
   - `client_secret`: Your client secret.
   - `Grant type`: Use the "client_credentials" grant type to request an access token.

**Example Request**
```bash
curl --location --request POST 'https://{site}.subscriptionflow.com/oauth/token' \
--header 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
--data-urlencode 'client_id=your_client_id' \
--data-urlencode 'client_secret=your_client_secret' \
--data-urlencode 'grant_type=client_credentials'
```

tags.name: Error Handling
tags.description:
SubscriptionFlow API provides error handling capabilities by using standard HTTP error codes and additional helpful parameters in its error responses. This page provides recommendations on effective error handling and a reference for all custom and standard error codes used by the API. **HTTP status codes** are used to indicate success or failure of an API call. The body of the response contains the details of the error in JSON format.

The standard http status codes used are

- **2XX**: The response code in this range implies the API call has succeeded. - **3XX**: This range is not used currently. - **4XX**: response codes indicate failure due to wrong input (i.e, client) side. As the usable list of http codes in the 4xx range is limited we predominantly use the 400 ( BAD REQUEST ) error code for client-side errors. - **5XX**: response codes indicate API request was valid but failed due to issues on the SubscriptionFlow server. The 500 ( INTERNAL SERVER ERROR ) error code is used for server-side errors.

tags.name: Customers
tags.description:
Represents a customer, which can be an individual or organization that subscribes to your products or services.
The customer resource associates with **subscriptions**, **card information**, and **billing addresses**.
The customer details include their ID, name, contact information, and any custom attributes you'd like to associate with them

tags.name: PaymentMethod
tags.description: A payment method represents a customer's payment information, such as credit cards, bank accounts, or other payment sources. Payment methods are associated with customers and can be used for processing transactions and subscriptions. In our system, payment methods are stored as references to the underlying payment gateway records (for example, a customer ID and payment method ID from Payment Gateway). These identifiers are securely saved and linked to the customer, allowing the system to perform recurring charges and subscription payments without storing sensitive payment details. All payment processing is handled directly by the gateway, ensuring compliance with security and PCI standards.

tags.name: Contacts
tags.description: A contact is a person associated with a customer. The contact details include the contact ID, name, email, phone, and status.

tags.name: Subscriptions
tags.description:
A SubscriptionFlow subscription connects a customer record to products/services.
It describes what the customer has signed up for and how often they're charged for it.
The essential components of a subscription are:

- Any product selected
- Any plan-item for a product
- Any coupons applied

tags.name: Usages
tags.description:
A usage is a record of a customer's usage of a product or service.
The usage details include the usage ID, name, customer ID, subscription ID, charge ID, status,
quantity, start date, end date, and description.

tags.name: Invoices
tags.description:
An invoice is a document that itemizes a customer's charges for products and services.
It is generated at the end of a billing cycle, which can be monthly, quarterly, or yearly.
The invoice details include the invoice ID, name, customer ID, invoice date, due date, and total amount.

tags.name: Transactions
tags.description:
A transaction is a record of a payment that has been processed for a customer.
The transaction details include the transaction ID, name, customer ID, transaction date, status, amount, and payment method.

The transaction status can be one of the following:
- **Paid**: The transaction is paid.
- **Failed**: The transaction failed.
- **Pending**: The transaction is pending.
- **Declined**: The transaction is declined.
- **Processing**: The transaction is processing.

tags.name: Products
tags.description:
A product is a good or service that you sell to your customers.
The product details include the product ID, name, type, description, SKU, effective start date, effective end date, and status.
Product have line items called **plan-items** that define the billing details for the product.

**Possible values for product type are**
- **Base Product**: A product that is sold as a standalone item.
- **Addon Service**: A product that is sold as an add-on to a base product.
- **Miscellaneous Product**: A product that is sold as a one-time item.

**Possible values for product status are**
- **Active**: The product is active.
- **Inactive**: The product is inactive.

tags.name: Plans
tags.description:
A plan is a set of billing details for a product.
The plan details include the plan ID, name, description, effective start date, effective end date, and status.
Plans have line items called **plan-items** that define the billing details for the plan.

**Possible values for plan status are**
- **Active**: The plan is active.
- **Inactive**: The plan is inactive.

> **Note:** A plan can be associated with multiple products.

tags.name: PlanPrice
tags.description:
A plan-item is a line item that defines the billing details for a product or plan.
The plan-item details include the plan-item ID, name, description, category, charge model, accounting account code,
list price, list price base, UOM, included units, default quantity, trigger condition, end date, billing timing,
billing period alignment, usage records rating option, billing period, billing period months weeks, billing month,
billing day type, billing week day, and status.

tags.name: Users
tags.description:
A user is a person who can access your SubscriptionFlow instance. The user details include the user ID, email,
first name, last name, role, status, address information, and locale.

tags.name: CreditNotes
tags.description:
A credit note is a document that itemizes a customer's credit for products and services.
It is generated at the end of a billing cycle, which can be monthly, quarterly, or yearly.
The credit note details include the credit note ID, name, customer ID, credit note date, due date, total amount, and status.

**Possible values for credit note status are**
- **Allocated**: The credit note is allocated.
- **Unallocated**: The credit note is unallocated.
- **Partially Allocated**: The credit note is partially allocated.
- **Cancelled**: The credit note is cancelled.
